questions.question: What is the maximum number of interfaces that can be aggregated with EtherChannel and PAgP?
questions.explanation: PAgP EtherChannel can bundle up to eight active interfaces.

correct.explanation: PAgP EtherChannel can bundle up to eight active interfaces.

incorrect.explanation: **2 interfaces** does not answer what is in this stem. PAgP EtherChannel can bundle up to eight active interfaces.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Selecting "2 interfaces" without matching the scenario constraint

incorrect.explanation: **16 interfaces** does not answer what is in this stem. PAgP EtherChannel can bundle up to eight active interfaces.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Selecting "16 interfaces" without matching the scenario constraint

incorrect.explanation: **4 interfaces** does not answer what is in this stem. PAgP EtherChannel can bundle up to eight active interfaces.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Selecting "4 interfaces" without matching the scenario constraint

answerReview.examTip: EtherChannel is one logical link to STP — **LACP (802.3ad)** is standards-based; PAgP is Cisco-proprietary.

questions.question: Which is a true statement about EtherChannel?
choices: EtherChannel works with 802.1Q to block the redundant links.
EtherChannel can aggregate multiple links with varying speed.
EtherChannel can aggregate interfaces across multiple stand- alone switches.
When configured, EtherChannel acts as a single layer 2 connection.
questions.explanation: EtherChannel acts as one logical Layer 2 link.

correct.explanation: EtherChannel acts as one logical Layer 2 link.

incorrect.explanation: **EtherChannel works with 802.1Q to block the redundant links.** is the wrong device role here. EtherChannel bundles links for bandwidth/redundancy — it is not inter-VLAN routing, MAC learning, or unknown-unicast flooding.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Dragging link-aggregation into an unrelated L2/L3 behavior question

incorrect.explanation: **EtherChannel can aggregate multiple links with varying speed.** is the wrong device role here. EtherChannel bundles links for bandwidth/redundancy — it is not inter-VLAN routing, MAC learning, or unknown-unicast flooding.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Dragging link-aggregation into an unrelated L2/L3 behavior question

incorrect.explanation: **EtherChannel can aggregate interfaces across multiple stand- alone switches.** is the wrong device role here. EtherChannel bundles links for bandwidth/redundancy — it is not inter-VLAN routing, MAC learning, or unknown-unicast flooding.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Dragging link-aggregation into an unrelated L2/L3 behavior question

answerReview.examTip: EtherChannel is one logical link to STP — **LACP (802.3ad)** is standards-based; PAgP is Cisco-proprietary.

questions.question: You need to form an EtherChannel between a VMware ESXi host and the switch. Which negotiation protocol will you choose?
questions.explanation: Use LACP for non-Cisco link aggregation compatibility.

correct.explanation: Use LACP for non-Cisco link aggregation compatibility.

incorrect.explanation: **EtherChannel** is the wrong device role here. EtherChannel bundles links for bandwidth/redundancy — it is not inter-VLAN routing, MAC learning, or unknown-unicast flooding.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Dragging link-aggregation into an unrelated L2/L3 behavior question

incorrect.explanation: **Channel Group** does not answer which in this stem. Use LACP for non-Cisco link aggregation compatibility.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Selecting "Channel Group" without matching the scenario constraint

incorrect.explanation: **PAgP** does not answer which in this stem. Use LACP for non-Cisco link aggregation compatibility.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Selecting "PAgP" without matching the scenario constraint

answerReview.examTip: Mark **inside/outside** first. PAT = many inside hosts sharing one **outside global** with unique ports.
